feedback.text: Greetings EU Commission, I am writing to you today to provide feedback about the Digital Fairness Act. I am a supporter of providing consumer rights and protections against unfair commercial practices. I would urge the Commission to consider the alteration or destruction of digital content that was purchased in this act. In particular, the alteration of agreements after sale and the deletion of products purchased. Altering agreements after sale, I argue, holds software hostage unless the consumer agrees as they are typically unable to access a "previous version"; the one they agreed to. As an example of how this can be used to negatively impact businesses, the game engine Unity wanted to alter their agreement to include a fee for every download of software created with it in January 2024. This move would have killed smaller development studios and incur costs to companies who would be targeted by malicious users. Just because this particular change did not end up happening does not mean that the next attempt will not succeed in harming users and industries. The next point is about deletion or loss of access to content purchased after the provider decides. It is perfectly understandable that support or sale of a product will eventually end. However, it should not bar the user from using software they have purchased. In the same way to how I could drive a Pontiac car even though Pontiac no longer exists, I should still be able to open software after the company goes under or wishes to stop supporting it. If the company does not have a end-of-life plan, this is a problem. Please refer to the movement Stop Killing Games as an example of how this can be prevented. In general, a company can stop producing the product or change how they sell their products yet this does not and should not affect the owner of a previously purchased product. Older software can still function for those who own it. Cars can still function even if the company who manufactured it goes bankrupt. Why should we allow software solutions to be held or deleted after sale? Should we allow Mercedes to prevent you to drive your automobile unless you agree to new terms after sale? Should Sony be allowed to take away your CD Player once they stop manufacturing it? For those of you who are still reading, look around you. Is every physical item in your home or office still being made or supported? Aren't you glad you own these things? Digital Fairness should also mean that the costumer owns the product they purchased.
